title: The Macro Minute:  Is the labor market about to fall off a cliff?

Happy Friday out there at Te42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Friday, May
2nd, 2025. As always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note. If you would like
to review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's key macro question is, is the
labor market about to fall off a cliff?
Our answers are unlikely. The April jobs
report indicated that the US labor
market remains resilient from an income
perspective, but employment growth is
slowing sharply as we expected. All
told, persistent leading indicators of
the business cycle currently indicate a
low to middling probability of a
developing recession in the US economy.
We continue to view a technical
recession as the highest probability
outcome, not an actual slash NBER
recession that features a broad-based
decline in consumption, production,
income, and investment. techno recession
being just multiple quarters of negative
growth that does not snowball into a
broader credit cycle. That said, the
labor market is likely to deteriorate
over the medium term, boosting consensus
recession odds in the process. Whether
asset markets can look through that
depends entirely on the messaging from
the Trump administration. The more they
allow historically elevated policy
uncertainty to linger, the higher
consensus recession odds will rise,
which may reflexively cause one to
occur. Worse, the Treasury bond market
blow up from early April confirmed one
of our biggest fears. The US lacks the
fiscal space to easily climb out of a
recession. The federal budget deficit,
which is currently 7%, could balloon to
10 to 15% of GDP at a time when foreign
creditors and investors would be less
inclined to capitalize the Treasury
market because of the Trump
administration's policy choices. And to
be clear, strategic ambiguity and
bullying trading partners and military
allies are indeed policy choices.
Moreover, they are policy choices that
risk dramatically and permanently
eroding the US's exorbitant privilege if
they cause a recession that leads to a
buyer strike in the Treasury market in
part due to the US's absurdly poor twin
deficit dynamics. It's a lot of
complicated stuff here, but uh hopefully
everyone is understanding that. And if
you don't understand that, uh, we have
our May 2025 macro scouting report
presentation coming out this afternoon,
published exclusively, uh, to 42 macro
clients. Uh, you know, we're going to
walk through all those c current account
and capital account dynamics uh, and
ultimately the risks we see both from a
medium-term perspective as it relates to
the economy and asset markets, but also
from a longer term perspective in terms
of where this is all heading in the
context of the fourth turning poly
crisis that we've been helping our
clients prepare for uh, for the la
better part of two years. So, um, you
know, if I sound like I'm wearing a
tinfoil hat, uh, you know, that that's
that's totally fine, but I'd rather put
the tinfoil hat on and help you guys
understand, uh, you know, paint a
credible road map using data. I think
there's 150 slides in this presentation
that I'm staring at. uh paint a credible
roadmap using data so that you know you
have some you know understanding of that
full distribution of probable uh
economic policy and market outcomes that
we talked about in yesterday's macro
minute. Transitioning to our 42 macro
dashboard as always we'll wrap up with a
question from our community. Uh this one
uh is near and dear to my heart because
it allows me to uh to geek out a little
bit uh for you guys. Um, so this one
says, uh, global macrois matrix strength
of signal, uh, has been dropping like a
rock o over the last week. Looks like
we're back in a market trying to figure
out what to do phase. Uh, fantastic, uh,
question here. Um, so let me pull up our
um, our global macro risk matrix here.
Uh, for those of you who may not be uh,
familiar with what we do uh, behind our
payw wall for our clients, one of the
most important things we do is now cast
a market regime. Uh, and the reason that
that's important is because investors
you you if you want to avoid FOMO, which
is fear of missing out, which causes
investors to buy cycle tops, or FOMO,
which is fear of more losses, which
causes investors to sell cycle lows, if
you want to avoid FOMO and FOMO, you
have to position for the market regime
as an investor. You don't have to
position for the market regime as a
retail investor. you can, you know, have
your shorts go up indefinitely or have
your loans go down indefinitely and no
one's going to tap you on the shoulder
until your bank until your brokerage
account runs out of money or your, you
know, your spouse, you know, divorces
you or whatever. Uh, but if you're an
institutional investor, which is where
this process comes from, then you must
position for the market regime because
eventually you're going to get tapped on
the shoulder and you won't be an
institutional investor anymore. You'll
be selling insurance. And so, we built
this system to make keep our buy side
clients on the right side of market
risk. And oh by the way it it really
works for retail investors too. So let
me um let me give you a little bit of
background about the system uh so I can
answer the question um cogently. Uh so
how the system works actually I can just
use my pin here. How the system works.
So what we're trying to do here is score
all the relevant markets that you know
major institutional investors traffic in
across uh stocks um volatility
commodities currencies and and fixed
income. Um, we're scoring all the
relevant the mo what we consider to be
the most the 42 most important markets
in the world from the perspective of
institutional investors uh market
exposures at least or market macro
market indicators. Uh we score each one
of those daily through the lens of its
own respective volatility adjusted
momentum signal. um you know we don't
have time to unpack exactly what that is
but then we tally up the score uh on a
daily on a daily frequency to identify
you know which regime is essentially
being shown the most love by asset
markets which we interpret to be the
market regime and so for example uh
let's you know let's pick on um you know
implied va correlations three-month
implied volatility correlations uh those
are currently bullish from the
perspective of their volatility adjusted
momentum signal uh historically implied
volatility correlation
um within the equity market have been
bullish in um inflation and deflation
which are the two riskoff regimes.
Inflation being a riskoff regime with an
inflationary bias, deflation being a
riskoff regime with a disinflationary
bias. And so you know the the current
bullish vamp signal for implied
volatility correlations is providing a
point for both inflation and deflation.
It doesn't know for an inflation or
deflation in isolation, but it knows
that historically it's been bullish in
inflation or deflation. So right now
it's assuming that this particular one
of those 42 indicators is particular is
is um is is assuming that we're in
inflation or deflation. Uh let's use
Bitcoin as an example here. Uh Bitcoin
is currently bullish from the set of
volatility just a momentum signal.
Historically Bitcoin most of its
performance is concentrated uh in
Goldilocks and reflation. Goldilocks
being a riskon market regime with a
disinflationary bias. Reflation being a
risk-on market regime with a
reflationary bias with an inflationary
bias. And so the current bullish VMs
condition, bullish volatility adjusted
momentum signal condition of Bitcoin is
currently contributing one point to both
Goldilocks and reflation. Again, Bitcoin
doesn't know if it's risk on with an
inflationary bias or risk off with an
infl sorry risk on with an inflationary
bias or risk on with a disinflationary
bias. It just knows that it's risk based
on its bullish V's condition. And so
we're tallying each of these markets
daily from that perspective to get a
sense of which markets, which of the
four regimes is actually, you know,
again, getting shown the most love
getting confirmed by the maximum number
of markets or not the maximum number,
but the the highest sum and share of
those confirming markets. And the
neutral signals in this in this process
don't count because there's no
conviction in that particular indicator.
So we're only tallying the bullish and
bearish signals on any given day. And
then we sum that up to identify which
regime, which of those four market
regimes we identified uh is getting the
maximum amount of uh the sorry the
highest sum and share of those
confirming markets. And right now
deflation uh is the highest uh sum and
share. There are 16 of the 42 markets
confirming deflation as opposed to 12
for Goldilocks, eight for uh reflation,
and 10 for inflation. And so we say okay
as long as that defla as long as
deflation remains the highest sum and
share we'll say we're still in a
deflation market regime. Now going back
to the question which I think is a
fantastic question obviously this is a
client that's been with us for a while
uh the strength of signal which is just
the total amount of of confirming
markets in any given day displayed on a
percentile basis with daily uh of date
with daily data going back to January
1998. The current total amount of
conviction that's being you know
generated by global macro markets right
now as evidenced by the cumulative
number of of signal being generated
across these four uh regimes is only in
the 23rd percentile. And so that you
know with daily data going back to
January 1998 and and so what that
essentially is saying is markets are
increasingly less convicted about what
regime they want to price in right now.
And you can see this represented pretty
clearly on this chart where we show the
versus the share of each the confirming
markets. Um the chart on the left shows
the sum. The chart on the right shows
the share. Whenever you see a limited
amount of dispersion between the
regimes, that tells you that markets
aren't really sure if we're pricing risk
on, risk off, or inflation, deflation.
Right now, markets are kind of in a
holding pattern right now from the
perspective of their medium-term
volatility just momentum signals across
the 42 markets that are contributing to
this uh to this process. And so um going
back to the question is essentially
saying that we could be on the you know
what this essentially says is we could
potentially be on the precipice of
regime change in asset markets i.e.
going from deflation to either
goldilocks, reflation or inflation. Uh
and obviously two of those three
outcomes is are bullish you know from
the perspective of a risk on market
regime and uh one of those potentially
is bearish. So uh you know this is a uh
really important I think asset markets
are very much at an important crossroads
right here. Uh this has been a very
important week uh both from an economic
signaling standpoint. We got the GDP
report, the PC report, the jobs report,
uh more jobless claims data. We got all
the the not the all for the max 7
company earnings. We got a ton of
information that markets are currently
digesting uh this week. And that in my
opinion is what's contributing to the
fact that global macro markets across
the 42 most important volatility
adjusted momentum signals to track in
the world from our perspective. uh
global macro markets are now kind of in
this holding pattern and trying to look
around say okay what's next what's the
next big macro catalyst where are we
going from here you know a lot of the
you know just where are we going from
here is really the question that asset
markets are a are asking right now and
in terms of this um in terms of our
process and the reason this stuff
matters right you know again you have to
position for the market regime to avoid
FOMO and FOMO that's the number one
reason we do what we do here from a
market regime now casting process uh you
don't want FOMO O in a raging bull
market in so much that you don't want
FOMO in a raging bare market. And so
just acknowledging what regime we're in
and and positioning your portfolio at
least at the very margins. You know, if
you're an institutional investor, but if
you're a retail investor, you know, you
have the flexibility in terms of your
account size and liquidity to reposition
your entire portfolio uh to take
advantage of what the market regime
signals are are signaling. Especially
when new regime change occurs. That's
when the most money is made is when you
get the signal and you immediately
respond to it in your portfolio because
it maximizes the number amount the the
total amount of days or the total amount
of weeks or months that you could
potentially participate in the market
regime. It gives you the best cost basis
to participate in the new market regime.
This is what we mean by staying on the
right side of market risk. uh and then
just historically uh if you um in terms
of the regime change statistics you know
uh because I think that's you know very
important to understand exactly you know
how frequently this process will
generate signal particularly meaningful
signal uh in your portfolio and what we
mean by meaningful signal is rorow phase
transitions row phase transition just
means you're going from risk on to risk
off or risk off to risk on that's what
really matters as it relates to staying
on the right side of market risk
institutional investors care if it's
disinflationary bias or infl
inflationary bias in terms of Goldilocks
versus reflation or inflation and deep
versus deflation because they're going
to make very, you know, specific factor
long short bets across a variety of
factors in their portfolio, you know, in
order to generate relative returns in
asset markets. That's a very
institutional, you know, sort of problem
set that we're trying to solve and and
and help our clients solve here on the
institutional risk management side. But
retail investors, all you really need to
know is are we risked on or risk off
period? And most importantly, you need
to know, okay, today is the day we're
going risk off or today is the day we're
going risk on so that you can do
something differently in your portfolio
to remain on the right side of market
risk. And and essentially, you know,
with data going back to J daily data
going back to January 1998, if you look
at over a trailing three-year, 5year, 10
year, and 20-year perspective, you
you're talking about basically, you
know, two rorow phase transitions per
year. So that means going from a bull
market, you know, if you're, let's say
you're in a bull market, that means you
go down into a meaningful enough
correction that you need to risk manage
to stay on the right side of market
risk. That would be one. And then you
break back out into the bull market.
That's two. So once a year essentially
what our data what our daily data
suggests with data going back to January
1998 in terms of this market regime
casting process what the data suggest is
there's really one material correction
per year that you need to risk manage in
order to stay on the right side of
market risk one you know so that you're
not you know risking potentially blowing
up your account uh in a potential crash
because you don't know if it's a crash
you know was there between a crash and
and a correction ahead on an Xanti basis
really nothing. On a X-ost basis you you
can tell the difference between a crash
and a correction but Xanti they both
look the same. So and so you need to do
something in order to um in order to to
give yourself the best to optimize uh
remaining on the right side of market
risk. So again there's only one material
enough correction per year in asset
markets to you know stay on the right
side of that's what our data confirms.
There's a lot of chop and a lot of noise
and most retail investors are trading
way more frequently than that. We we try
to avoid trading at all if we don't have
to here at 42 Macro. We don't want to
trade at all. I'd rather not trade. But
most retail investors are constantly
responding to, you know, fundamental
news, podcasts, talking heads on TV,
Twitter, LinkedIn. They're constantly
getting what they think is signal, but
it's mostly noise. The data tells you
that most it's most of it's noise. you
only really need to make two decisions
per year to remain on the right side of
market risk generally speaking obviously
these are these are averages but just
you know again these averages are
persistent across a trailing three year
trailing five year trailing 10 year and
trailing 20-y year uh uh sample so in my
opinion I think you you you can trust
that uh and then I would say if we're in

because usually we get a policy
response. Um you know, that that that
that prevents that. Um but that kind of
